title: Specify what makes a product 'disruptive'
summary: AI-generated copy calls products 'disruptive' without explaining which market convention is being overturned or how.
instruction: If using 'disruptive', specify the market convention being overturned, the underserved segment, and how the product differs from incumbent solutions. Describe the disruption mechanism, not just the label.
rationale: Clayton Christensen's theory defines disruption as serving overlooked markets with simpler, cheaper, or more accessible alternatives. Casual use of 'disruptive' for any new product empties the term of meaning. A specific description of the market change ('Makes professional design tools accessible to non-designers') is more credible and informative.

violations: 'A disruptive platform for modern teams' without specifying the market or approach.
'Disrupting the [industry] space' as a standalone claim.
controls: 'Makes professional design tools accessible to product managers and developers who don't have design training.'
'Disrupting the $50B CAD market by bringing real-time collaboration to 3D modeling — a category that has had zero native multiplayer support.'
borderline: A genuinely innovative product entering a market with entrenched incumbents — 'disruptive' may be accurate but should still be backed by specific market analysis.

exceptions.description: Academic or analytical writing referencing Christensen's theory of disruption. Market analysis reports using 'disruptive' as a defined category term.
examples: Harvard Business Review article on disruption theory

detector.signalDescription: Find 'disruptive', 'disruption', 'disrupt' in marketing copy. Check for specific market context, incumbent description, or underserved-segment identification nearby.
limitations: Determining whether a product genuinely disrupts a market requires domain knowledge beyond static analysis.
Academic uses of 'disruption' as a defined theoretical term should not be flagged.

scenarios: A genuinely innovative product with a well-articulated disruption thesis.
Academic or analytical writing using 'disruption' as a defined theory term.
Investor pitch decks where 'disruption' is a category expectation.
mitigations: Flag only egregious cases where 'disruptive' appears without any market context in the same section. Accept well-researched disruption claims.

copyPattern.replacementStrategy: Replace 'Disrupt [industry]' with specific market thesis: the incumbent limitation being overcome, the overlooked segment being served, and the mechanism that makes the new approach different.

fixturePlan.description: A landing page saying 'Disrupting the enterprise software space' with no market specifics.
Same page with 'Bringing real-time collaboration to an industry that has relied on email attachments for 20 years.'
A truly novel product entering a stagnant market with a clear thesis — 'disruptive' may be accurate with supporting context.
